name: Order arrows for linear multistep methods
subsection_title: Maximum order for a convergent k-step method
statement_text:
An A-stable linear multistep method cannot have order
greater than 2.




             Figure 443(i)    Order arrows for order 2 BDF method




            Figure 443(ii)     Order arrows for order 3 BDF method
proof_text:
Proof. If the order were greater than 2, there would be more than three up
arrows emanating from the origin. At least three of these up arrows would
come out in the positive direction (or possibly would be tangential to the
imaginary axis). Since there is only one pole, at least two of these arrows
would cross the imaginary axis (or be tangential to it). Hence, the stability
region does not include all of the imaginary axis and the method is not A-
stable.
proof_latex:
\begin{proof}
If the order were greater than 2, there would be more than three up arrows emanating from the origin. At least three of these up arrows would come out in the positive direction (or possibly would be tangential to the imaginary axis). Since there is only one pole, at least two of these arrows would cross the imaginary axis (or be tangential to it). Hence, the stability region does not include all of the imaginary axis and the method is not A-stable.

We can make this result more precise by obtaining a bound on the error constant for second order A-stable methods. The result yields an optimal role for the second order Adams--Moulton method, for which the error constant is $-\frac{1}{12}$, because
\[
\exp(z) - \frac{1 + \frac{1}{2}z}{1 - \frac{1}{2}z} = -\frac{1}{12}z^{3} + O(z^{4}).
\]
It is not possible to obtain a positive error constant amongst A-stable second order methods, and it is not possible to obtain an error constant smaller in magnitude than for the one-step Adams--Moulton method. To prove the result we use, in place of $\exp(z)$, the special stability function $(1 + \frac{1}{2}z)/(1 - \frac{1}{2}z)$ in forming a relative stability function.
\end{proof}
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context} The theorem concerns linear multistep methods, where stability and order are analyzed via a complex relation \(\Phi(w e^{z}, z) = 0\). Order arrows and the principal sheet of the Riemann surface are used to study behavior near zero, analogous to one-step methods, leading to a bound on order for A-stable methods.

Variables:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \(\Phi\): \(\Phi(w e^{z}, z) = 0\) defines a relationship between complex numbers \(z\) and \(w\).
    \item \(w\): complex number related to \(z\) via \(\Phi(w e^{z}, z) = 0\); on order arrows, \(w\) is real and positive.
    \item \(z\): complex number related to \(w\) via \(\Phi(w e^{z}, z) = 0\).
    \item order arrows: set of points for which \(w\) is real and positive; up arrows have \(w \in (1, \infty)\), down arrows have \(w \in [0, 1)\).
    \item principal sheet: neighbourhood of \((0, 1)\) where the relationship between \(z\) and \(w\) is injective, behaving as though \(w\) is a function of \(z\).
    \item \(p\): order of the method; on the principal sheet, \(w e^{z} = e^{z} + O(z^{p+1})\).
\end{itemize}
